summary: My name is Jonathan Worth Daniels, born on April 26, 1902, in Raleigh, North Carolina. I grew up in my grandfather's house near Shaw University, in a predominantly black neighborhood. My family background is quite rich, with connections to the Civil War and Southern politics, and my father was a significant figure in North Carolina's Democratic Party and newspaper world. I had a complex but respectful relationship with my father, especially regarding race relations and politics.

I was born in Raleigh, North Carolina, right at the corner of South and Blount Street, across from Shaw University. Raleigh was the place where my grandfather built our family home shortly after the Civil War, and though the neighborhood deteriorated over time, it was a happy place with wonderful neighbors. Raleigh's social and racial dynamics deeply influenced my upbringing and family history.
cues: corner of South and Blount Street

summary: Shaw University was right across the street from where I was born in Raleigh. Growing up near Shaw University placed me in a unique position within a predominantly black neighborhood, which shaped much of my early social experience. It was part of the environment where my family lived and interacted with our neighbors.

The American Civil War was a defining backdrop for my family history. My grandfather, Jonathan Worth, was a major in the Confederate States Army and served as treasurer during the war. The war's aftermath shaped my grandfather's political career and the family's standing in North Carolina, and it influenced my father's upbringing and values.

Little Washington, North Carolina, was where my father's father worked as a ship carpenter before the Civil War. He left for Rhode Island because he opposed slavery but returned to North Carolina to marry my grandmother. Later, when my father became Secretary of the Navy, Little Washington tried to honor him, though my grandmother was skeptical of the town's welcome.

Rhode Island was where my paternal grandfather went to live and work because he did not believe in slavery. Despite this, he returned to North Carolina to marry my grandmother before the Civil War began. His time in Rhode Island reflects the complex personal and regional loyalties in my family history.

My grandfather was a skilled ship carpenter who helped build the Merrimack, which later became the CSS Virginia during the Civil War. This connection to the famous ironclad ship is a notable part of my family's maritime and military heritage. It symbolizes the intersection of craftsmanship and conflict in our history.

Goldsboro, North Carolina, was where my grandmother lived with my Uncle Frank when my father visited after becoming Secretary of the Navy. It was during this visit that my grandmother expressed her skepticism about the town's welcome for my father, reflecting complex family and social dynamics. Goldsboro thus figures into my family's later history and relationships.

My mother's family originally came from Quaker roots, and my grandfather Jonathan Worth was a Quaker who opposed secession during the Civil War. This background influenced his election as governor by the white population after the war, though he was later removed from office. Despite this heritage, my grandmother was quite different from the typical Quaker stereotype, being a strong and dominant woman.

Jonathan Worth was my maternal grandfather and served as governor of North Carolina after the Civil War. He was a Quaker and opposed secession, which helped him gain office, though he was removed by the military commander General Canby. His letters were compiled by De Roulhac Hamilton, and he was a moderate figure during a turbulent time.
Daniel E. Sickles was not directly mentioned in my family story or experiences, so I have no personal connection or recollection related to him.

William Woods Holden was appointed governor of North Carolina after my grandfather Jonathan Worth was removed by General Canby. He was a significant figure during Reconstruction, and his governorship marked a shift in political control. Holden's role is part of the political context surrounding my grandfather's tenure.

Worth Bagley was my uncle and a celebrated figure as the first officer to fall in the Spanish–American War. He was a great football player at the United States Naval Academy, and his death was seen as symbolic, being the first blood shed by a Confederate officer's son. Though largely forgotten now, his legacy was important to my grandmother, who regarded him with great pride.

The United States Naval Academy was where my uncle Worth Bagley excelled as a football player before his death in the Spanish–American War. This institution was part of his path to becoming a naval officer and a family hero. The Academy represents a connection between my family and national military service.

The Spanish–American War was a significant event for my family because my uncle Worth Bagley was the first officer to fall in that conflict. His death was widely noted at the time, symbolizing the participation of Confederate descendants in the United States military. This war marked a moment of pride and sorrow for my family.

My grandfather Jonathan Worth served as a major in the Confederate States Army during the American Civil War. He was also the treasurer during the war and a moderate figure who hoped to placate the conquering Yankees after the conflict. His service and political role deeply influenced my family's history and identity.

Sharon was the name of my great-grandfather Worth's plantation near Raleigh, which later became a black public school. This place connects my family to the history of slavery and education in North Carolina. It symbolizes the transition from antebellum plantation life to post-war social changes.

De Roulhac Hamilton was a historian who compiled two volumes of letters from my grandfather Jonathan Worth. His work helped preserve and interpret my grandfather's thoughts and experiences during the Civil War era. I consider Hamilton's scholarship important for understanding my family's past.

My mother was a Presbyterian, and although my father was a Methodist, they maintained their separate church memberships. They would alternate attending services together, showing a respectful but distinct religious practice in our family. This dynamic reflected the religious diversity and tolerance in my upbringing.

My father was a Methodist and held to his church membership even though my mother was Presbyterian. They respected each other's faiths and attended services together alternately but never joined the other's church. This arrangement was part of the religious fabric of my family life.

My father was deeply involved with the Democratic Party in North Carolina and was chosen to travel throughout the South to devise legislation to disenfranchise illiterate blacks while preserving white voting rights. He worked closely with Governor Charles Brantley Aycock and was influential in the white supremacy campaigns of the early 20th century. Despite the harshness of those times, he was known for his gentleness and fairness in personal relations.

Louisiana was one of the states my father visited as part of his work for the Democratic Party to develop constitutional methods to disenfranchise illiterate black voters while protecting illiterate white voters. He brought back legislation from Louisiana and other places that was adopted in North Carolina. This work was part of the racial politics of the South in the early 20th century.

Charles Brantley Aycock was a close friend of my father and my Uncle Frank's law partner. They shared a strong interest in education for both races and worked together politically in North Carolina. Aycock was also involved in the white supremacy campaigns, but he and my father opposed dividing tax revenues racially for education.

The News & Observer was the newspaper my father edited and later I took over. It was a strong voice in North Carolina politics, especially during the white supremacy campaigns. My father's editorial stance was often fierce, though personally he was gentle, and I inherited the paper with some differences in views, especially on Prohibition and civil rights.

Wesley Hoover was our next-door neighbor in Raleigh, a black man who became fairly wealthy running a saloon, which my father disapproved of but still respected. My grandmother taught him to read and write, and despite social taboos, my father treated him with courtesy, though never calling him 'Mr. Hoover.' Wesley even acted as a secret bodyguard for my father during a tense political campaign.
